excerpt_en: Hey there, it's MaSU from FOMUS! Today I'll be talking about the theme of "Thinking About My Approach." ▼Challenges Starting Next Month So, today is Thursday. The weekend is almost here. That means two days of spreading FOMUS out in the field.
body_en:
Hey there, it's MaSU from FOMUS!

Today I'll be talking about the theme of "Thinking About My Approach."

▼Challenges Starting Next Month

So, today is Thursday.

The weekend is almost here.

That means two days of spreading FOMUS out in the field.

This week too, from Tuesday through today,

I've been putting out a call for photo models

on FOMUS's Instagram.

FOMUS's Instagram doesn't have a huge reach,

so once again, I'm running Instagram story ads.

Over three days, about 30 people

sent in offers for Masu Photo shoots,

and I've been replying to most of them, though not all.

What I noticed from the Masu Photo shoot I did two weeks ago is that

people with private accounts

can actually reach a more core audience by sharing within their private space.

Both in Japan and Malaysia,

I used to have a bias that private accounts had no influence power and weren't suited for Masu Photo,

but it might've been just by chance -- last time I accepted offers from private account holders too, and I actually got messages from their friends, so it did have an effect.

That said, I don't think "anyone should be able to do a Masu Photo" -- I screen people as much as possible.

I look at their Instagram accounts,

and if it's a public account, I can immediately check their face and what they post.

If it's a private account, I ask them to "send me your photo" to verify their identity before proceeding.

Honestly, when it comes to receiving offers,

it would reduce my workload if people submitted their face photo and reason for applying upfront,

so as I'm writing this article,

I just decided that next time I'll have people submit their photo and reason for applying before accepting their offer.

#UpdatingMyApproachWhileWriting

February Is for Experimenting

Honestly,

in the first week, I already proved that it's possible to shoot Masu Photo with models in Cebu.

Then, in the second week,

I planned an AI art workshop,

but not enough people showed up, so it was postponed.

And now, in the third week,

I'm applying what I learned in week one by actively responding to offers from private account holders as well.

Also, last time I had every model wear the masu accessory "FOMUS Parure,"

but 18 and 19-year-olds who look young don't quite match the target demographic for FOMUS Parure, so I realized there's no need to force them to wear it.

I think it's better to have those with a more mature vibe wear the FOMUS Parure, and have others do a regular Masu Photo shoot -- that way the brand positioning is clearer.

*Parure looks great on her

With FOMUS's Masu Photo,

it's important that anyone can freely express themselves,

so I reply to anyone I feel even slightly inclined to shoot,

and I hope to make the shoots happen.

Starting in March, I plan to take a different approach

to Masu Photo shoots in Cebu.

I tried searching Instagram for the "aspiring idol" types I spotted at IT Park last week,

but I couldn't find them, so I'm not sure what to do...

I already have five shoots confirmed for Saturday,

so I'm planning to ask them about the local modeling scene as well.

I also place a lot of importance on

how much I can expand the FOMUS brand during my short three months in Cebu.

Should I spread it wide and shallow, or narrow and deep?

In reality, since it's a three-month period (with school on weekdays),

I effectively have about 25 days of outside activity,

so spreading wide is inherently difficult.

I'm hoping to expand the FOMUS brand

mainly within Cebu City.

By the way, I've released three Masu Photo collections so far,

and this time I'll be making a "Masu Photo Collection in Cebu" too.

https://masuphoto.theshop.jp/secret_ec/secret_ec_auths/login

The online version will be free.

I'll also make a physical booklet since it doubles as a FOMUS portfolio.

Based on the Malaysia photo collection, I can put one together with at least 10 people.

I think by this week

I'll surpass the minimum number of Masu Photo subjects needed for the collection.

That's a relief for now!

That said, I'm not satisfied yet,

so I'll keep pushing forward!

excerpt_en: Hey there, it's MaSU from FOMUS! Today I'll be talking about the theme of "ZUMBA Experience!" ▼ZUMBA at School! Today is Friday. I only had classes in the morning, and in the afternoon, instead of regular classes, there was a ZUMBA
body_en:
Hey there, it's MaSU from FOMUS!

Today I'll be talking about the theme of "ZUMBA Experience!"

▼ZUMBA at School!

Today is Friday.

I only had classes in the morning,

and in the afternoon, instead of regular classes,

there was a ZUMBA class.

It's one of the school's activities.

ZUMBA is

a dance fitness exercise created by fusing Latin music and dance.

It's a dynamic and exciting interval training-style workout

that combines fast and slow rhythm songs, burning a lot of calories, so of course it's also effective for fat burning.

This ZUMBA class was open to anyone,

and more than 30 of us students and teachers participated.

For about an hour, students and teachers did ZUMBA together,

and we danced our hearts out.

We did it outside, and since the temperature is Cebu-level,

it was incredibly hot.

Even so, everyone was going all out while having fun,

and I enjoyed it too.

For those who might be heading to language school or reading this article, let me tell you this:

You should definitely participate in activities like this.

There are several reasons.

First, it's easy to make friends.

Second, it leads to interaction with the teachers.

And also, you simply get to have experiences you can't have in Japan.

In fact,

this was my first time ever experiencing ZUMBA,

and I'm really grateful that they provide these kinds of experiences that I'd never seek out on my own.

Thanks to that,

I can share fresh content with all of you through this article.

I actually wanted to take photos,

but the dancing was more intense than I expected, with no breaks,

so I couldn't get my camera out.

Someone else took photos though,

so I'll share them once I get them.

Evening Drinks and Socializing

Since it's Friday, we had the weekly graduation ceremony.

My roommate started at the same time as me

and graduated after completing his three-week program.

So we headed to a restaurant within a 10-minute walk from school,

got some chicken lechon for takeout,

* The full name of "lechon" is "lechon baboy," which means "whole roasted pig." It's a famous local Filipino dish, and at the local festival events called "fiesta," they almost always serve lechon -- it's hugely popular among the locals.

Then we celebrated the graduation over beers at a bar.

After that, we bar-hopped to another bar we'd been to before,

where we had beers and balut (the gross egg thing) and hung out.

Little by little,

I'm getting friendlier with the bar owner,

so I'm planning to start introducing masu there.

*It's not exactly the kind of place where you can just whip out a masu

*Not even sure if they'd carry it

*First things first: gotta spend some money there

And that's how

the Friday of week three turned out -- morning classes, ZUMBA, and a drinking session. It was a pretty fulfilling day.

0e720bd93bb04406b06b6767a70ff6ab.mov

*A video someone took from behind

excerpt_en: Hey there, it's MaSU from FOMUS! Today I'll be talking about the theme of "Working on Improvements." ▼Masu Photo Shoot in Cebu So, today is Saturday, which means it's a day for spreading FOMUS. This time too, I've been recruiting on Ins
body_en:
Hey there, it's MaSU from FOMUS!

Today I'll be talking about the theme of "Working on Improvements."

▼Masu Photo Shoot in Cebu

So, today is Saturday,

which means it's a day for spreading FOMUS.

This time too, I've been recruiting on Instagram since Wednesday.

Over three days, more than 50 local people reached out to me.

Once again, some had zero followers or private accounts with no face shown,

leaving me wondering, "Can I even shoot with this person??"

Of course, I'm also negotiating shooting conditions,

so not everyone says yes.

Plus, there's a limit to how many people I can shoot in a day.

That's because I want to shoot in a setting where we can properly interact, so I only shoot with one or two people at a time.

Honestly, if it were people I'd shot with many times before,

I could do group shoots,

but that's not how things work in Cebu.

The reason I value the interaction is

so that people can properly learn about

the masu brand "FOMUS."

What kind of passion drives it.

How the Masu Photos from the shoot will be used.

I need to convey these things during the shoot interaction

or after the shoot (if there's time).

That said,

shoots don't always go smoothly.

I'm writing this

20 minutes past today's first shoot time.

Yeah, 20 minutes and they're still not here lol

So I'm using this time

to write this article.

Honestly, in Japan,

if you're going to be late, you'd send a message saying "I'll be X minutes late" -- but that doesn't happen here!

*Depends on the person, of course

Even when I ask "What time will you arrive?", there's no reply,

which makes it hard to figure out what to do with that time.

In my case, I can use the time like this

to write articles for my subscribers,

and jot down what I'm honestly feeling right now, so it works out.

But still, being kept waiting doesn't feel great!

Reminders Are a Must

Actually, I had five shoots scheduled for today,

and I ran the ads from Wednesday

with Friday morning as the deadline.

But someone who confirmed their schedule on Wednesday ended up forgetting.

This was also my mistake for not sending a reminder the day before.

In both Japan and Cebu, I used to send reminders the day before,

but this week I sent the reminder on the morning of the shoot day.

That was the mistake.

What Can I Do Now to Improve?

So, for future shoots,

what can I do to minimize

these kinds of frustrations?

I just have to think it through and take action.

With the understanding that being late is pretty much the norm here...

(1) Send a reminder message the day before the shoot

(2) Let them know in advance to contact me at least 15 minutes before if they'll be late

(3) If there's no contact for 30+ minutes past the shoot time, the shoot is canceled

Furthermore,

as I wrote in the previous article,

For shoot offers,

require applicants to send their "reason for applying" and "a photo of themselves" together

as a condition.

I think I should add that.

Honestly, during my shoots in Malaysia,

I never had to deal with being kept waiting like this.

*Nobody was late

So I assumed this was just how things are in Southeast Asia.

But different countries have different cultures.

Also, since I have Masu Photo shoots planned in Europe this year, I think I should establish these shooting standards now

so I can handle shoots more efficiently going forward.

*By the way, for today's 1 PM slot,

two people were scheduled,

and one forgot about the shoot.

The other hadn't contacted me even 40 minutes past the shoot time.

That's the situation lol

For someone like me who hates

both keeping people waiting

and being kept waiting,

it was a pretty tough time.

I'll apply the lessons from this experience going forward.

But Still... lol

I'm writing this part after the shoots.

The whirlwind of shoots successfully wrapped up.

As for the person who didn't respond,

they messaged an hour later: "Sorry, I just arrived."

This time, since I anticipated things like this could happen, I'd added a condition to the shoot terms:

"Other models may be shooting at the same time."

So I had the late arrival join the next slot (every 60 minutes).

In the end, the second person was also about 30 minutes late.

I shot the first person one hour late,

and finished the second person's shoot in 30 minutes.

The third person actually came on time.

*Only one person came on time, by the way

*The other three were more than 30 minutes late

With five shoots today (well, four since one person bailed), having even one person run late threw off the entire schedule.

Of course, finishing a shoot within the 30-60 minute time limit is easy enough, but the communication afterward is what I really value.

The fact that those conversation times got shortened

was definitely a disappointing part.

Even so, I can tell from talking with them that they find FOMUS interesting, and they enjoy the photo shoots, so there's absolutely meaning in doing Masu Photo.

And if FOMUS can reach people

I can't reach on my own,

through the local models,

that impact is tremendous.

In the end, the tough moments

are quickly forgotten.

*I don't dwell on hardships

That's exactly why I need to put those tough moments into words right when they happen, and properly think about preventive measures.

When you take action, you make lots of discoveries.

Once again, feeling the cultural differences,

enjoying the interactions with local models,

it was a day of spreading FOMUS!

https://youtu.be/CYq_KWV--a4?si=0_utNSmMslq4f4MG

excerpt_en: Hey there, it's MaSU from FOMUS! Today I'll be talking about the theme of "Joining an International Exchange Event!" ▼International Exchange Event! Today, after doing one Masu Photo shoot, I attended an international exchange event run by a Japanese company.
body_en:
Hey there, it's MaSU from FOMUS!

Today I'll be talking about the theme of "Joining an International Exchange Event!"

▼International Exchange Event!

Today, after doing one Masu Photo shoot, I attended an international exchange event run by a Japanese company.

The Masu Photo shoot went great!

*Today, a friend wanted to see the shooting process,

so they came along as my assistant.

*So I had them film my shooting process too.

Anyway, about the international exchange event --

The participation requirement was "must be able to speak Japanese or English"

and I had no idea what kind of people would show up,

but I decided to just go for it!

Of course, I was genuinely interested in the event itself,

but I also figured there would definitely be a chance for self-introductions,

which would be an opportunity to let people know about

the masu brand "FOMUS."

So, what actually happened was...

There were SO many people!!

It was mainly locals from Cebu and Japanese people,

roughly a 50-50 split.

Most were in their 20s and 30s.

During the roughly three-hour event,

we split into several groups for self-introductions

and discussions on assigned topics.

I'm not really a fan of being given discussion topics,

but our group ignored the assigned topic

and just talked in English about whatever interested each of us.

In Cebu,

this was my first time attending an event

without having to do my own outreach,

and it was great that so many people I couldn't gather on my own were there

to interact with -- that's the beauty of events organized by locals.

Most of the Japanese people were there for language study,

and since Cebu has lots of language schools,

it's probably relatively easy to attract attendees.

This time, I got a lot of people to know about me

and about FOMUS.

As expected, masu

gets people excited no matter which country you're in,

and people seem curious when you tell them

you started your own brand and are out there building it.

Because I Changed My Environment

Today marks three full weeks,

and starting tomorrow is week four.

Little by little,

I feel like my English listening skills are improving.

That's probably because

I'm exposed to English every day,

so my ears are getting used to it.

Thanks to that, my Masu Photo shoots

now involve more conversation than when I was shooting in Malaysia.

That said,

there are still many things I can't catch and lots of words I don't know,

so it's not like I understand everything.

Honestly, a three-month study abroad in Cebu isn't cheap.

But because I put up the money

and placed myself in an English-immersion environment,

not only is my English improving,

but I'm also meeting lots of new people.

On top of that, when it comes to overseas marketing,

this might just be because of who I am,

but through hands-on experience,

I'm learning more efficient marketing approaches.

I'm dedicating three precious months of my twenties

to Cebu,

so I want to absorb everything I can

and turn it into fuel for my growth.

After the event,

I went out for food with some Filipino friends I'd just made.

We went to everyone's favorite -- "Jollibee."

*Think of it as the McDonald's of the Philippines

About eight of us enjoyed dinner and good conversation.

Moments like these

wouldn't have happened if I hadn't put myself out there,

so I'm sure the importance of taking action is coming through loud and clear! lol

At the international exchange event,

communication skills like speaking and listening

are incredibly important.

In my case,

I developed these skills later in life rather than being born with them,

but combined with this environment,

I feel like they're being polished even further.

With that said,

I'm going to dive into week four,

keep learning, and enjoy the connections!

https://www.notion.so

excerpt_en: Hey there, it's MaSU from FOMUS! Today I'll be talking about the theme of "My Roommate Changed." ▼I've Got New Roommates Day 22 in Cebu. Week four starts today. I'm staying in the language school dormitory, and
body_en:
Hey there, it's MaSU from FOMUS!

Today I'll be talking about the theme of "My Roommate Changed."

▼I've Got New Roommates

Day 22 in Cebu. Week four starts today.

I'm staying in the language school dormitory,

and up until now, I haven't talked much about my roommates.

That's because

my roommates were Japanese.

As people, they were super funny and fun to be around, but what I'd originally imagined for language school was a rich international experience with English flying around everywhere,

so the reality was a little different from my expectations.

That's how I spent those three weeks,

(though this life was also really enjoyable)

and at this point,

the first two roommates graduated and left,

leaving just me,

and new roommates moved in this week.

The two are Chinese and Korean.

And with me there too,

our room-share life with people from three different countries has begun.

We introduced ourselves,

and both turned out to be interesting people, so that's great.

Also, since I'm staying for three months,

as I showed in a recent YouTube video,

I'd gotten a bath mat, toilet slippers, and Resesh (a deodorizer) to make the room more comfortable,

and I explained all that to them too.

Different countries mean different cultures (same goes for photo shoots),

so I don't plan to set strict rules,

but the minimum basics

are set up so they're obvious just by looking, without needing to be told.

In week four, the dorm life I'd been imagining has finally begun.

▼Customizing My Teachers

As for school, I'm now in week four and fully used to the classes. Switching teachers is second nature now.

There was one class period where

I hadn't been lucky with the teacher,

but the teacher in the room next to mine

(the rooms are glass-walled so you can see faces)

had the kind of face that made me personally want to take their class, so I requested them.

Starting today, I have class with that teacher.

Since it was the first day, we did introductions

and discussed how to structure the lessons.

I told them I want to do business English and daily conversation training, without textbooks -- just "conversation-focused."

They gave the OK, of course,

so the format is: they ask me questions about business,

I answer,

and if my response doesn't come across clearly, they explain and correct me.

Since I'll be heavily using English for business in practice going forward, having someone correct me now is incredibly valuable.

That said, if I made all my classes conversation-only,

the fundamentals would suffer,

so I also have teachers who carefully cover the basics.

Because I have a decent understanding of my weaknesses and what I need to strengthen in English,

I chose the teachers, tailored the lesson styles,

and picked the ones I felt had the strongest communication skills.

Maybe it's the effect of all that,

but I feel like I leveled up significantly just today.

Also, since I was immersed in English all weekend, my ears have gotten even more used to it.

▼Not Just Roommates

On top of that,

lots of new students started this week,

and I've been interacting with them frequently,

so the time I spend using English at school has increased even more.

I talked with someone from Taiwan,

and I was able to tell them about last year's Taiwan cycling project

where I rode 1,125 km around the island,

and since I remember the names of the regions,

the way I connect with Taiwanese people has completely changed.

https://fomus-nft.my.canva.site/taiwan-gps-run

Once again, I realized how important it is

to actually visit places and see them with your own eyes.

Unfortunately,

I haven't been to China or South Korea yet,

so I don't have firsthand info about my roommates' countries, but that just means I ask them lots of questions and interact that way.

So,

every week brings new updates,

and I'm genuinely enjoying this life.

On the work front too,

I'm handling Japanese projects remotely,

and even while in Cebu, I'm creating content that will become long-term assets.

One of those is short-form videos.

https://vt.tiktok.com/ZSFkFaff1/

In this format,

I'm creating lyric videos featuring FOMUS's character "KUKU,"

reaching audiences worldwide,

with the goal of getting as many people as possible to know KUKU.

Even in Cebu, when I bring up KUKU, people get really excited about it -- that's because Japanese anime and manga are so widespread and popular here.

It's made me even more eager to get to work on KUKU anime and movie production.

That said, there's an order to things,

so for now I'll focus on building a solid foundation,

becoming ready to handle whatever comes,

and keep moving forward.

title_en: Expression in the Age of Social Media
excerpt_en: Hey there, it's MaSU from FOMUS! Today I'll be talking about the theme of "Expression in the Age of Social Media." ▼"Short Movie" Project Launches Up until now, I've been writing about my daily activities in Cebu, but honestly the weekday language school content is running thin.
body_en:
Hey there, it's MaSU from FOMUS!

Today I'll be talking about the theme of "Expression in the Age of Social Media."

▼"Short Movie" Project Launches

Up until now,

I've been writing about my daily activities in Cebu,

but honestly the weekday language school content is running thin.

The reason is simple:

I'm repeating the same routine.

Writing articles requires

new input every day,

and that's hard when I'm doing the same things.

That said,

it's not like I'm failing to gain input and output during my Cebu life -- far from it.

In my case, I have my own business,

so since arriving in Cebu, I've been taking a "door-to-door" approach for on-the-ground activities -- going out and winning people over one by one.

Masu Photo is a perfect example.

Every other week,

I run Instagram ads from Wednesday to Friday

to attract people.

Then, for those who could be models,

I reach out,

and on my days off (weekends),

I go meet them in person,

take photos,

and share FOMUS's vision and passion.

FOMUS has many people who freely

use masu and FOMUS content in their own activities,

and there's plenty that can be done remotely too.

One of those things is

the "Short Movie" project.

At the end of last year,

I released a rules explanation video for FOMUS's card game "SILVA,"

and a person named Fuku-san edited that video.

We got to talking about what drives each of us in our work.

That conversation happened in December,

and we agreed that we'd launch something exciting together in the new year -- and finally, that project has kicked off!

▼How to Spread the Character "KUKU"

Now, shifting gears a bit,

FOMUS has a character called "KUKU."

KUKU was created to reach audiences that FOMUS and the traditional Japanese craft of masu hadn't reached before,

using a character IP to get everyone from adults to the elderly to know about it.

The ultimate goal of the KUKU project

is an animated feature film.

To get a character to reach as many people as possible,

turning it into an anime is incredibly powerful.

This isn't just my impression from Japan alone --

having traveled to and interacted with people in various countries,

I feel this firsthand.

To make that happen,

I need to raise funds,

and in 2023 I was planning to release an NFT collection, but market conditions weren't great, so I postponed it.

So,

I've been thinking about how to develop KUKU for a long time.

Then, out of that December conversation,

combining FOMUS's strengths --

traditional Japanese crafts, characters, a broad user base, knowledge of AI and NFTs --

with Fuku-san's video editing skills,

a collaborative project was born.

That's the KUKU short movies.

To explain simply,

we're releasing roughly 30-second lyric videos as short movies.

FOMUS has the character but was struggling with how to deliver it,

and by combining Fuku-san's lyric video and editing skills, we can break into the current golden age of short-form video.

That said, if we obsess over going viral,

AI and other technologies will outpace us,

so we're different from other TikTokers.

Normally, it might be better to make lyric videos with currently trending songs,

but there's a risk of people losing interest quickly,

so we decided to start with "timeless classics."

Then, once we've built a solid base of regular viewers,

we'll start mixing in trending songs.

So, we've released the first one.

https://vt.tiktok.com/ZSFkFaff1/

I briefly mentioned the short movies in the previous article, but here I've made the "why" behind this project much clearer.

Being in Cebu and working during gaps in language school

actually turns out to be a pretty good setup.

By the way, for the short movies, all the character assets, background art, and posting are handled by FOMUS.

I do the background creation and posting manually,

but each takes only about 10-20 minutes,

so it's no problem at all.

From Cebu and Japan, we're creating strong content,

broadcasting it to the world, and working to get KUKU known by as many people as possible.

So this time,

I switched things up a bit

and wrote about my business activities in Cebu.

Going forward,

I'll be weaving in articles like this bit by bit.

This is a Cebu activity log, after all,

so I'm just sharing what I'm working on, as it is.

excerpt_en: Hey there, it's MaSU from FOMUS! Today I'll be talking about the theme of "Going on the Offensive!" ▼What I Learned Over One Month So, once again I'll focus on business topics. Since arriving in Cebu on February 4th,
body_en:
Hey there, it's MaSU from FOMUS!

Today I'll be talking about the theme of "Going on the Offensive!"

▼What I Learned Over One Month

So, once again I'll focus on business topics.

Since arriving in Cebu on February 4th,

I've been using weekday after-school hours and weekends

to spread FOMUS,

and the weekly "Masu Photo" ads

have been showing consistent results.

Plus,

I've figured out the answer to

"what kind of approach works best?"

So,

after this week's story ads in Cebu,

I'll be pausing the Masu Photo ads for now.

The reason is simple: "I've got the data I needed."

Furthermore,

I've already done over 10 Masu Photo shoots in Cebu,

which exceeds the minimum number of models needed

for the photo collection I'll be creating in May.

In other words,

the photo collection goal was cleared within this one month.

So,

here's where the real story begins.

▼Going on the Offensive

Up until now, I've been using ads to attract

and shoot with interested people,

but next I want to focus on

shooting with professional models and people

in the entertainment industry.

So I've started using Instagram

to search for models and actresses based in Cebu and the rest of the Philippines.

That said, this week was a lot of trial and error.

It was hard to find models.

Searching "cebumodel" didn't turn up much,

and I started to wonder,

do models even exist in Cebu?

But through lots of trying,

I found a way to search,

and managed to find about 100 people.

On top of that, one of my language school friends has a male teacher whose Instagram I'd exchanged, and he follows a lot of female models and portrait subjects.

From there, I surfed through their friends and followers and found lots more models.

From here, it's about reaching out to each one individually

and waiting for responses.

This approach is the same one I used when I first started as a photographer, looking for people to shoot for my Instagram -- reaching out to people all across Japan.

Basically, finding local models through hashtags and contacting them one by one.

Even though it's online,

it's a pretty labor-intensive process.

Back when I was doing this in Japan,

if three out of ten people responded, that was good.

From the perspective of Cebu models,

I'm a foreigner.

If the response rate was already low between Japanese people,

will they even accept an offer from a foreigner?

I'll find out starting tomorrow when I begin sending messages.

If I can get shoots, I'll shoot.

Up until now, it's been "passive" advertising,

but starting in March,

I'm taking an aggressive approach to finding models.

I'm able to take this strategy

because I already hit the minimum number of Masu Photo shoots this past month.

In other words, I have breathing room when it comes to shoots.

While I have this cushion,

I'm going to take an approach that has a high chance of failure

but massive impact if it succeeds.

▼Search Skills Matter

What I'm reminded of once again

is the importance of search skills,

whether on YouTube or Instagram.

If you can research efficiently,

you save that much time and money.

Right now, a college student sitting next to me is helping search for models. He just started using Instagram at this point,

and through helping with my model search, his search skills have skyrocketed.

It's "just" model searching,

but model searching nonetheless.

"Search skills" can be trained!! lol

So, this time the story was about moving the Cebu Masu Photo shoots to the next phase!

Actually, this morning I also had my monthly level check test as a one-month student.

I'll get the test results tomorrow,

so I'll talk about that next time!

https://www.notion.so

excerpt_en: Hey there, it's MaSU from FOMUS! Today I'll be talking about the theme of "I Took a Test." ▼A Monthly Test So, this time I want to talk about language school. Day 25 in Cebu. At my school, CELLA,
body_en:
Hey there, it's MaSU from FOMUS!

Today I'll be talking about the theme of "I Took a Test."

▼A Monthly Test

So, this time I want to talk about language school.

Day 25 in Cebu.

At my school, CELLA,

there's a progress test on Wednesday of the fourth week.

In other words,

I took the second test in the same format

as the initial placement test.

So,

it serves as a benchmark for measuring my English improvement over this past month.

Here's what the test looked like:

Once again, there were tests for speaking, listening, reading, and writing.

For speaking, it's measured through actual conversation with a teacher and reading passages aloud.

The other skills

were your standard written test format.

At this point, let me reveal

my initial scores...

  • Listening: 27
  • Reading: 28
  • Writing: 15
  • Speaking: 35

That's right.

Since it's out of 80 points,

those are pretty rough numbers, right?

But that's exactly why I came here to study English,

so this time is crucial for me.

Now, how did the second test go?

  • Listening: 31
  • Reading: 38
  • Writing: 44
  • Speaking: 47

Those were the results!

The bottom line is

all four skills did improve.

Score changes:

  • Listening: +4
  • Reading: +10
  • Writing: +29
  • Speaking: +12

However,

as expected, reading and listening are weak...

Especially listening -- even with smartphones and AI advancing,

they're not yet extensions of yourself in that way,

so you need to understand it on your own.

And my score is low.

In other words,

there's still a lot I don't understand.

What I personally feel is that

I've gotten too used to Japanese-accented English,

and I can't handle the native-level words in listening tests, so I fail to understand.

Also, vocabulary and grammar knowledge --

these are still clearly lacking.

I plan to work hard on these areas

over the remaining two months.

That said,

speaking and writing showed remarkable improvement.

For speaking,

once I've said something once, it sticks,

so I'll keep talking as much as possible to sharpen it.

▼Customizing My Classes

So,

now that my current level has been measured,

in terms of test scores, my English is average.

40 out of 80.

Of course, I'm not studying just to raise my test scores, but it's a clear benchmark.

Next time, I want to get all skills up to at least 55.

To achieve that,

I feel the need to make some changes to my classes.

My challenges are:

1. I don't fully understand English grammar, so I can't construct sentences well and end up communicating with just individual words

2. I simply don't know enough vocabulary

3. I haven't been able to dedicate much time to English prep and review outside of class (I've been spending time on work and gathering information)

There are other smaller issues,

but these are the big ones.

Regarding #3, my business --

a big project finally wrapped up,

so I should be able to allocate a bit more time to English.

And for #1, grammar,

I've asked one teacher

to go back and teach me grammar from scratch.

*You can tell them what you want to study

Also, to properly work on grammar,

I've resumed the "writing" class I'd previously dropped.

*Up until now I'd been doing conversation-focused classes, but I switched back today

*Strangely, my writing score wasn't low even though I hadn't been studying it

*A question has come up: if I can't write it, can I really listen or speak it either?

As for #2, not knowing enough vocabulary --

this is something I just have to work on

during spare moments on my own.

Knowing more words means more ways to express myself,

which improves conversation quality,

and in listening, not knowing a word can derail comprehension, so it can only help.

Tomorrow marks the start of March,

and this next month I'm going into full-on English mode.

The big FOMUS online project is done,

and I've streamlined other tasks as much as possible.

And starting next week,

since I'll mainly be reaching out to professional models, the weekend Masu Photo shoots will slow down.

*I don't expect getting permission to shoot professional models to be easy.

The conditions for focusing on improving my English are falling into place.

Also, I've been keeping up with the running I mentioned before -- every day since then.

Today I ran 5 km in 33 minutes.

Taking care of my health while

keeping up with my activities.

title_en: I Envied Someone for the First Time in a While
excerpt_en: 2024/03/1 Hey there, it's MaSU from FOMUS! Today I'll be talking about the theme of "I Envied Someone for the First Time in a While." ▼Being a College Student Is Nice So, today is Friday, and I attended the graduation ceremony for this week's graduates.
body_en:
2024/03/1

Hey there, it's MaSU from FOMUS!

Today I'll be talking about the theme of "I Envied Someone for the First Time in a While."

▼Being a College Student Is Nice

So, today is Friday,

and I attended the graduation ceremony for this week's graduates.

This week, about 15 college students

who started at the same time as me

are graduating after one month of study abroad.

Normally, if they're all from the same university, they tend to stick together, which means less interaction with other people.

But there was one person who reached out to me, a nobody of sorts.

Let's call him "D" here.

Over about a month, we interacted frequently,

using English as much as possible in our conversations.

He was the kind of person who was liked by both students and teachers, with a rich sensitivity.

We often went out to eat together,

and during free study time,

we'd sit at the same table working on different things.

Today was his last day.

Several graduates were selected to give a graduation speech in English,

and he was one of them.

*I'll probably give a speech when it's my turn to graduate too

At that moment,

I felt "envious" for the first time in a really long while.

Here's why:

before he could even start his graduation speech, his emotions overflowed and he cried.

In front of all those students and teachers,

he was so moved that he broke down in tears.

I found that incredibly beautiful.

I basically never envy anyone in life,

but seeing someone with such a pure heart, letting rich emotions pour out like that --

As a businessman,

maybe that's not how you're supposed to be,

but looking at him as a fellow human being,

"envy" was the first emotion that came to mind.

Lately, I've been

viewing things from a detached perspective

(I've always been a bit on the cool side)

and I don't really let emotions show in those kinds of settings.

But he showed pure, unfiltered feelings.

He has something I don't (or maybe something I threw away somewhere along the way), and he was shining.

That's what drew out this rare feeling of envy.

I was a college student just a few years ago too,

going through similar experiences,

but I couldn't feel those same emotions back then.

"The grass is always greener on the other side," I suppose.

*This almost never happens to me,

so I'm writing my raw emotions as they are in this blog.

Your twenties as a college student come only once in life.

If you're reading this,

I highly recommend going abroad to study, even if just for one month.

▼Party Night

That evening, including D,

a group of graduating friends and non-graduating students -- about eight of us -- went out for dinner.

Koreans, Japanese, and Chinese,

all enjoying delicious food together.

Then for round two, we headed to a bar near the language school.

When we arrived, the school's head teacher

and other teachers were already drinking there.

We sat at a different table and got our drinks.

*Beer, of course

Since many of the group hadn't tried balut (the duck egg with the almost-hatched embryo inside), I bought a bunch and we all ate them together.

I'd already had it three times by then,

so I was used to it,

but watching the first-timers' reactions was hilarious.

After that, I went over to the teachers' table

to join their conversation.

The reason was simple:

I wanted to be part of the adults' conversation.

That said, my listening and speaking skills aren't that high yet, so I couldn't catch everything, but if I can practice English outside of class time, that's a win.

So I actively joined in.

Thanks to that,

lots of teachers learned my name.

I think this is possible because I've traveled all over Japan,

diving into groups of complete strangers without any hesitation -- that mindset I've developed really helps.

My school's teachers are close-knit,

and they often drink together on Friday nights,

so being able to increase my interaction with teachers at times like this is great for me.

I've already done a Masu Photo shoot with the head of the school,

so they know I run a traditional Japanese craft brand

and that I'm a photographer.

*Nothing left to chance

Drinking together really is great.

This bar also has karaoke,

so everyone was freely singing and dancing.

It was an absolute blast!

▼That Feeling of "I Want to Talk to This Person More!"

Lastly,

let me wrap up with some casual chat --

the kind of stuff that probably won't be all that useful. *Feel free to skip this

At the drinking session with the teachers,

there were some teachers I'd never seen before,

and among them

was someone whose face was absolutely my type.

Of course, the urge to "talk to her" kicked in.

I went over without a second thought.

English level didn't matter --

it was pure "I want to talk to this person!" energy.

Maybe it was the alcohol too,

but in that moment I was in the zone,

performing above my usual English level,

and I had a great conversation.

"I want to talk to this person" is

an incredibly powerful motivator for learning English,

so I've got one more reason to push myself.

*It might not be the purest motivation, but it's a huge source of power

And so,

Friday night

turned out to be an incredibly fun and fulfilling day.

That wraps up week four of classes.

One month flew by in no time.

I'll keep having fun while studying English.

excerpt_en: 2024/03/2 Hey there, it's MaSU from FOMUS! Today I'll be talking about the theme of "Refresh Day." ▼Off to the Casino Saturday. School's closed, so it's free time. That means it's a day for spreading FOMUS, but
body_en:
2024/03/2

Hey there, it's MaSU from FOMUS!

Today I'll be talking about the theme of "Refresh Day."

▼Off to the Casino

Saturday.

School's closed, so it's free time.

That means it's a day for spreading FOMUS,

but no Masu Photo shoot requests came in for Saturday, so I ended up with a full day off.

*In exchange, Sunday is packed with shoots

So,

after having brunch at school (on days off, we get two meals a day), I headed to the casino with a friend I'd gotten close with here.

Cebu has several casinos,

and I went as a way to unwind after one month.

Online sources said

a passport was required,

but when I actually went, that wasn't the case --

just a bag check and I was in.

That said, I didn't come to make money.

I just came to play,

so I wasn't about to bet big.

I took only my 500 pesos (about 1,300 yen)

as betting money

and played roulette.

After about 30 minutes,

I realized I was up to 2,480 pesos (about 6,500 yen)!

Up about 2,000 pesos.

*Just luck

This was my first time at a casino

since Macau on my college graduation trip,

and it really is a fun place.

*Not gonna get hooked though

The Cebu casino I went to

is inside the upscale Waterfront hotel,

and it was a really clean place.

Hotels that have a money-generating system (a casino) are probably thriving economically, just like in Macau.

It was nice to see that firsthand.

*No cigarette smell either

*It was a really clean casino

I wouldn't go to a casino alone,

but for some light fun with friends,

it seems like just the right thing.

▼Walking the Talk

In article #20, "I Started a Morning Routine," I wrote about my morning runs, and I've kept it up every day since.

Every day, 20-30 minutes,

running 3-5 km.

*12 days in a row

That said,

my stamina and strength are still at a beginner level,

and I'm nowhere near ready to run 42 km.

So I plan to keep training, but running every day does leave my body sore and tight.

So after the casino, I went to the spa I'd been to before.

This time I got a 2-hour oil massage for 850 pesos (about 2,200 yen).

In Japan, a 2-hour massage

would easily cost over 10,000 yen,

but that's Cebu for you.

A veteran masseuse worked on me,

and it felt absolutely amazing.

My legs feel so much better.

With about 4-5 shoots lined up for tomorrow,

being able to get this refresh time in was huge.

Then, I had a light dinner near the spa at IT Park,

took a Grab back to the dorm.

After that, I did the weekly upload of KUKU's short movie

to YouTube, TikTok, and Instagram,

and now I'm writing this article.

https://vt.tiktok.com/ZSFAfbPMW/

Alright, Sunday will be a full day of shoots.

I'm going to give it my all with the Masu Photo shoots

and make sure everyone has a great time with FOMUS!
